analogy: Think of "Law and Social Justice" through the chapter's own opening images: The Making of the National Movement: 1870s–1947 8 In the previous chapters, we have looked at:  The British conquest of territories, and takeover of kingdoms  Introduction of new laws and administrative institutions  Changes in the lives of peasants and tribals  Educational... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Law and Social Justice is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that the Making of the National Movement: 1870s–1947 8 In the previous chapters, we have looked at:  The British conquest of territories, and takeover of kingdoms  Introduction of new laws and administrative institutions  Changes in the lives of peasants and tribals  Educational changes in the nineteenth century  Debates regarding the condition of women  Challenges to the caste system  Social and religious reform  The revolt of 1857 and its aftermath  The decline of crafts and growth of industries On the basis of what you have read about these issues, do you think Indians were discontented with British rule?

The text explains that if so, how were different groups and classes dissatisfied?

The text explains that 1 – Police teargas demonstrators during the Quit India movement

The text explains that 94 OUR PASTS – III The Emergence of Nationalism The above-mentioned developments led the people to ask a crucial question: what is this country of India and for whom is it meant?

The text explains that the answer that gradually emerged was: India was the people of India – all the people irrespective of class, colour, caste, creed, language, or gender.

The text explains that and the country, its resources and systems, were meant for all of them.

The text explains that with this answer came the awareness that the British were exercising control over the resources of India and the lives of its people, and until this control was ended, India could not be for Indians.

The text explains that this consciousness began to be clearly stated by the political associations formed after 1850, especially those that came into being in the 1870s and 1880s.

The text explains that most of these were led by English-educated professionals such as lawyers.

The text explains that the more important ones were the Poona Sarvajanik Sabha, the Indian Association, the Madras Mahajan Sabha, the Bombay Presidency Association, and of course the Indian National Congress.

The text explains that the literal meaning of “sarvajanik” is “of or for all the people” (sarva = all + janik = of the people).

The text explains that though many of these associations functioned in specific parts of the country, their goals were stated as the goals of all the people of India, not those of any one region, community or class.

The text explains that they worked with the idea that the people should be sovereign – a modern consciousness and a key feature of nationalism.

The text explains that in other words, they believed that the Indian people should be empowered to take decisions regarding their affairs.

The text explains that the dissatisfaction with British rule intensified in the 1870s and 1880s.

The text explains that the Arms Act was passed in 1878, disallowing Indians from possessing arms.

The text explains that repeal – To undo law; to officially end the validity of something such as a law Source 2 Activity What problems regarding the early Congress does this comment highlight? 

The text explains that in coastal Andhra and interior Tamil Nadu, liquor shops were picketed.

The text explains that they were protesting because the colonial state Source 3 The eternal law of suffering W h a t d i d M a h a t m a Gandhi mean by ahimsa ( n o n - v i o l e n c e ) ?

The text explains that 102 OUR PASTS – III had restricted their use of forest resources in various ways.

The text explains that why do you think they were in favour of Gandhiji?  Source 4 Fig.

The text explains that it announced 16 August 1946 as “Direct Action Day”.

The text explains that how did they propose to struggle against British rule?

The text explains that how was the politics of the Radicals within the Congress different from that of the Moderates?

The text explains that discuss the various forms that the Non-Cooperation Movement took in different parts of India.

The text explains that why did Gandhiji choose to break the salt law?

The text explains that discuss those developments of the 1937–47 period that led to the creation of Pakistan.

The text explains that find out how the national movement was organised in your city, district, area or state.

The text explains that find out more about the life and work of any two participants or leaders of the national movement and write a short essay about them.

The text explains that you may choose a person not mentioned in this chapter.

The text explains that let’s imagine Imagine that you are involved in the Indian national movement.

The text explains that based on your reading of this chapter, briefly discuss your preferred methods of struggle and your vision of a free India.

Source: NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III, Chapter 8: Law and Social Justice

schemes.name: The Arms Act
schemes.point: The Arms Act was passed in 1878, disallowing Indians from possessing arms. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch8 pg 1)

schemes.name: In
schemes.point: In the same year, the Vernacular Press Act was also enacted in an effort to silence those who were critical of the government. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch8 pg 1)

schemes.name: The Act
schemes.point: The Act allowed the government to confiscate the assets of newspapers including their printing presses if the newspapers published anything that was found “objectionable”. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch8 pg 1)

schemes.name: In
schemes.point: In 1883, there was a furore over the attempt by the government to introduce the Ilbert Bill. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch8 pg 1)

schemes.name: But
schemes.point: But when white opposition forced the government to withdraw the bill, Indians were enraged. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch8 pg 1)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 94 OUR PASTS – III and THE MAKING OF THE NATIONAL MOVEMENT: 1870s –1947 95 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Law and Social Justice; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Law and Social Justice through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 94 OUR PASTS – III and THE MAKING OF THE NATIONAL MOVEMENT: 1870s –1947 95 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Law and Social Justice, which statement is supported by the source text about 98 OUR PASTS – III?
options: In the same year, the Vernacular Press Act was also enacted in an effort to silence those who were critical of the government.
The Act allowed the government to confiscate the assets of newspapers including their printing presses if the newspapers published anything that was found “objectionable”.
In 1883, there was a furore over the attempt by the government to introduce the Ilbert Bill.
The bill provided for the trial of British or European persons by Indians, and sought equality between British and Indian judges in the country.
